---
title: "The diaspora multiplier"
subtitle: "Curaçao at the 2026 World Cup and what the resident-population unit cannot see"
author:
  - name: Rendell de Kort
    orcid: 0000-0000-0000-0000
    email: rendell@c-economics.com
    affiliations:
      - name: Cornerstone Economics
      - name: Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam
date: today
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
abstract: |
  Curaçao opens its first World Cup against Germany on 14 June 2026. With
  about 150,000 residents it is the smallest country ever to qualify for
  the men's tournament, less than half the population of the previous
  record holder, Iceland in 2018. At world rank 82 the qualification
  reads as anomalous: a low-ranked country reaching the final stage
  despite a population gap with every other finalist. The natural
  inference is that the FIFA ranking is missing quality the
  resident-population framing cannot see.

  The data say otherwise. Across 202 nations with complete data, FIFA rank
  moves systematically with resident population, and population alone
  explains close to a third of the global variation in rank. The ranking
  is doing roughly what population alone predicts. Curaçao is over-performing
  its population by about 80 rank places. The residual cluster Curaçao sits
  in includes Uruguay, Croatia, Denmark, Portugal, Belgium, and the Netherlands.
  Curaçao and the Netherlands occupy the same residual position on the
  population-rank axis, one at 150,000 residents and one at 17.5 million.

  The squad behind the rank is where the question goes. The Curaçao men's
  senior squad carries a Transfermarkt market value
  of 184 euros per resident, level with Iceland at 192 euros and
  roughly four times the median of our 13-country comparison panel.
  Curaçao reaches Iceland's per-resident level on forty per cent of
  Iceland's population. Every player in the current call-up develops
  abroad in a professional league. FIFA's parent-and-grandparent
  eligibility rule extends the recruitable pool from 150,000 residents
  to a multi-generation diaspora concentrated in Dutch and other
  European professional football.

  We are explicit that this is a single case in a single qualification
  cycle, and we do not over-read it. What the Curaçao result raises,
  regardless of how the 2026 tournament unfolds, is a methodological
  question: in small states with substantial diasporas, the
  resident-population unit may not be the right reference for measuring
  competitive capacity.
keywords:
  - small-state economics
  - diaspora
  - football
  - measurement
  - FIFA ranking
jel-codes:
  - F22
  - Z22
  - O15
ce-paper-number: "WP-2026-01"
ce-version: "1.0.0"
ce-doi: "10.5281/zenodo.20126987"
bibliography: references.bib
csl: chicago-author-date.csl
format:
  pdf: default
  html: default
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Introduction

Curaçao opens its first World Cup against Germany on 14 June 2026. On the FIFA ranking
it sits at 82. With a resident population near 150,000 it is the smallest country ever
to qualify for the men's tournament, by a factor of two over Iceland in 2018. Two
features of the case look anomalous at first reading. The world rank is below the
population's natural peer group. The qualification itself sits below the population
gap to every other finalist in the field. The natural reading is that the ranking
under-measures Curaçao's actual football quality.

That reading is wrong. Across a panel of 202 nations, FIFA rank tracks resident
population with a coefficient that is large in size, narrow in confidence, and
significant at p below ten to the minus eighteen. Larger countries rank better, with
a slope of roughly fifteen rank places gained per natural-log unit of population. The
relationship is the noisiest in the upper-middle ranks where many countries cluster,
but the population term alone explains close to a third of the variance in rank
across the world. The FIFA ranking captures most of what population alone predicts
about national-team quality. This is consistent with the long-standing finding in
the sports-economics literature that aggregate national-team performance is well
explained by economic and demographic mass [@milanovic2005globalization;
@hoffmann2002socio; @houston2002income; @yamamura2009technology]. The ranking is
doing the work the public gives it credit for.

What it does not do, and was never going to do, is generate squad quality. Curaçao's
position in the residual distribution of that population-rank relationship is the
seventh strongest in the world, alongside Uruguay, Croatia, Denmark, Portugal,
Belgium and the Netherlands. The country ranks roughly eighty places higher than
its 150,000 residents alone predict. That residual is the squad. The Curaçao
men's national squad carries a market value per resident of €184, level with
Iceland's €192 on forty per cent of Iceland's population. The squad's club
distribution is unambiguous: every senior player
develops in a foreign professional league, and not one in the current call-up is
based on Curaçao itself. The squad is a
diaspora pyramid that exceeds what the resident base could carry on its own.

This paper sets out the empirical pattern, locates Curaçao within it, and returns to
what diaspora explains and what it does not. Section 2 describes the FIFA ranking and
the population-rank relationship across the global panel. Section 3 specifies the
diaspora mechanism, links it to FIFA eligibility rules, and shows where the pool of
foreign-trained players sits in the four Aruba and Curaçao squads. Section 4 documents the data and
sources. Section 5 reports the findings, including the cross-country regression on
202 countries, the residual cluster around Curaçao, and the per-capita squad value.
Section 6 discusses what the result implies for the small-state-football literature
and where the diaspora story stops being the explanation. Section 7 sets out
limitations.

# Population, ranking, and the global pattern

The FIFA men's world ranking is an Elo-style points system rebuilt in 2018 around the
sum of expected and actual results. Each fixture produces a points exchange that
depends on the pre-match rating gap between the two sides, the importance of the
match in the FIFA calendar, and a confederation-strength multiplier. Matches in the
World Cup and continental finals carry the heaviest weight; friendlies the lightest.
The system is designed to converge on each side's true rating across many fixtures.
For most of the federations in the panel, the convergence works.

Where the system has known limitations is at the ends of the distribution and where
match volume is thin. Small confederation members play few competitive fixtures per
cycle, draw most of those fixtures against regional opponents in similar rating
bands, and accumulate or shed points slowly. The resulting rank is informative but
the marginal sensitivity to a single result is low. Reviewers of the 2018 reform have
documented these volume effects in the predictive-accuracy literature
[@lasek2013predictive; @lasek2016improve].

What the public discussion of small-state qualifications often misses is that the
ranking is, on average, doing close to the work population alone would predict. The
broader pattern is consistent with what @mak2026voetbal finds at the European-club
scale: a country's UEFA club coefficient rises with national GDP, with Cyprus, the
Faroe Islands and Portugal as the visible upward outliers. We are working with a
different unit of analysis, national teams rather than clubs, and a different
predictor, resident population rather than GDP, but the broad shape is the same.
Economic and demographic mass predict competitive football performance; the
analytical interest lives in the residuals.
We estimate the population-rank relationship for the 202 FIFA-listed countries with
non-missing United Nations migrant stock and World Bank population data. The
specification is

$$
\text{rank}_i = \alpha + \beta \, \log(\text{population}_i) + \varepsilon_i
$$

with rank coded so that lower numbers indicate higher quality. The estimated slope is
$\hat\beta = -15.2$ ($p < 10^{-18}$), the intercept is 342, and adjusted $R^2$ is
0.31. Adding log diaspora-per-capita as a second regressor changes the population
coefficient by less than one unit and produces a marginally significant additional
effect of the right sign ($\hat\beta_{\text{dia/pc}} = -4.16$, $p = 0.16$). The
interaction of population and diaspora is statistically indistinguishable from zero.
What this tells us is straightforward. Population is a strong descriptive predictor
of rank, the FIFA ranking is broadly internally consistent, and a country's rank
residual against population is the sharper analytical object than the rank itself.

```{r}
#| label: fig-pop-vs-rank-global
#| fig-cap: "FIFA rank versus log resident population, 202 countries. Rank 1 is best; the y-axis is reversed so the strongest teams sit at the top. Curaçao is highlighted in coral; the trend line is rank ~ log(population) with 95% confidence interval. Source: FIFA men's ranking 10 May 2026; World Bank WDI population (latest available)."
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig6_global_pop_vs_rank.png")
```

# Diaspora and the eligible-pool extension

Population is the natural denominator for predicting football quality only as long as
the pool of eligible players for a national team is the resident population. FIFA
eligibility rules unlock a second source. A player is eligible to play for a country
if they were born there, if a parent or grandparent was born there, or if they have
lived there for the relevant residency period set by the Statutes [@fifa2024statutes].
Crucially, a player remains eligible for a national team after switching residence,
and the senior-cap-tied option allows mid-career switches in well-defined conditions
that broaden the realised pool further [@holmes2011fluid]. For most of the world this
set of rules is slack. Most national teams draw a clear majority of their senior
squad from resident-trained players, and the eligibility-pool extension changes the
headline number by a small share of the squad.

For small states with substantial diasporas, the rules bind differently. The
diaspora-resident ratio acts as a multiplier on the population denominator. Where
diaspora pools sit in countries with deep professional football pyramids, the
extension is not just numerical but compositional. Foreign-trained eligible players
enter the squad already developed at higher tiers than the domestic system can
produce. This dynamic is part of a broader literature on football labour migration
and the international division of football labour that has documented the flow of
elite talent from peripheral states into a small number of host professional pyramids
[@magee2002world; @poli2010understanding; @darby2007out]. That literature has mostly
framed the flow as brain drain from the sending federations. With the eligibility
rules operative, the same flow does something else: it builds an eligibility pool
the sending federation can call on, and the trained-abroad talent shows up in the
sending federation's national output. We are looking at the same migration, with the
federation eligibility rules read in.

The football case is a measurable instance of a broader claim in Caribbean diaspora
studies. @guadeloupe2026reweaving, accepting the Chair of Public Anthropology of
Kingdom Relations at the University of Amsterdam, argues that the Dutch Caribbean
islands and the Netherlands cannot be read as separate worlds with distinct native
cultures because the life trajectories of singular people interweave the Kingdom on
an everyday level. The argument extends earlier ethnographic work on Afro-Antillean
identity in Dutch popular culture [@guadeloupe2022black] and connects to a longer
tradition in postcolonial migration and cultural-identity scholarship that treats
diaspora not as displacement from a settled home but as a constitutive condition of
identity itself [@hall1990cultural; @oostindie2011postcolonial; @gilroy1993black]. The
diaspora-fed pyramid we describe is one quantitative shape of that interweaving.
Curaçaoan families with one branch in Willemstad and another in Rotterdam already
know what an interwoven Kingdom looks like. The paper puts a number on it. The extreme end of this pattern is the case where the
squad is fully diaspora-trained and the resident base contributes none of the senior
call-ups. Among
the four Aruba and Curaçao national teams in the data, this is the standing condition for
the Curaçao men's senior squad: zero of the most recent thirty-two confirmed call-ups
play their club football on the island. The same compositional reading shows up in
slightly weaker form for Cabo Verde, where the bulk of the senior pool develops in
the Portuguese and Dutch systems, and for Suriname, where the heritage pool with the
Netherlands is structurally similar to Curaçao's.

The same population, when the diaspora extends the pool that way, supports a squad
that the resident base alone could not. Section 5 quantifies this through the
per-resident squad market value and reports the regression of rank on population
across all 202 panel countries. The mechanism is descriptively visible in the four
Aruba and Curaçao squads in figure 2 and in the residual distribution in figure 6.

```{r}
#| label: fig-squad-location
#| fig-cap: "Composition of the four Aruba and Curaçao national-team squads by where players play their club football, most recent confirmed call-ups. The Curaçao men's squad has zero players based on the island. Bonaire, the third ABC island, has men's and women's national teams in CONCACAF/CFU but is not a FIFA member and is excluded from this comparison. Source: Cornerstone Economics ABC squads dataset, iter 1, April 2026."
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig2_squad_location.png")
```

# Data and method

The empirical work draws on four sources. FIFA's published men's world ranking
provides current rank and total ranking points for 211 federations as of 10 May 2026
[@fifa2026ranking]. World Bank World Development Indicators give resident populations
for the most recent available year (2023 or 2024 in most cases) [@wdi2025]. The
United Nations Department of Economic and Social Affairs International Migrant Stock
2024 provides the bilateral migrant stock matrix from which we recover diaspora as
the sum of country-of-origin migrants resident outside the country [@undesa2024].
Squad market values come from Transfermarkt's national-team headers, accessed on 10
May 2026 for thirteen focal countries selected for descriptive contrast: Curaçao plus
Cabo Verde, Albania, Suriname, Iceland, Malta, Luxembourg, Montenegro, North
Macedonia, Jamaica, Trinidad and Tobago, Panama, and Haiti [@transfermarkt2026]. The
focal-country selection covers Curaçao's small-state-with-diaspora category, the
two CONCACAF World Cup co-hosts that are not the United States, the European peers
that have qualified or come close in recent cycles, and the African and Caribbean
mid-table cases that complete the diaspora-ratio range. Transfermarkt valuations are
crowd-sourced from a community of contributors and reflect expected fee on the open
transfer market rather than current performance; the validity literature has
documented both the aggregate accuracy and the systematic biases of this measure
[@herm2014crowd; @coates2022wisdom; @muller2017beyond; @peeters2018testing]. We use
the aggregate squad-level total rather than individual valuations, which the
literature finds more robust. Squad-tier composition for the four Aruba and Curaçao
national teams comes from the Cornerstone Economics ABC squads dataset, iteration 1,
April 2026. Bonaire, the third island in the ABC grouping, has men's and women's
national teams affiliated with CONCACAF and the Caribbean Football Union but is not
a FIFA member; its squads are not part of the FIFA-ranking analysis and are excluded
from the data here.

The analytical strategy proceeds at two scales. The cross-country regression of rank
on log population runs on 202 countries with non-missing United Nations migrant stock
and World Bank population data. We extend this with log diaspora-per-capita as an
additional regressor, with the interaction term, and with log diaspora as a third
regressor to test absolute-scale effects against the per-capita specification.
Confederation fixed effects were considered but excluded from the headline
specification because confederation membership and population are not orthogonal in
the panel. The descriptive analysis uses the thirteen focal countries to compute
per-resident Transfermarkt squad value and to relate squad composition to the
per-capita result.

The reproducibility chain is fully scripted. Five R scripts in the repository
(`analysis/01_data_pull.R` through `analysis/05_models.R`, with `01b`, `01c`, `02b`,
`05b` extending the global panel) take the four hand-curated input files in
`data/raw/` to the regression results table and the figures in this paper. The
diaspora-source CSV records each value's source URL with a verification flag; the
script refuses to write the diaspora panel until every row is flagged verified.

# Findings

Three results sit at the centre of the paper. We report each with its empirical
shape rather than a single headline number.

The first is the global population-rank relationship. Across 202 panel countries
with complete population and United Nations migrant stock data, FIFA rank is
predicted by log population at a coefficient of $-15.2$ ($p = 2.3 \times 10^{-18}$)
with adjusted $R^2$ of 0.31 (specification G1, table 1). Adding log
diaspora-per-capita as a second regressor leaves the population coefficient
essentially unchanged, increases the adjusted $R^2$ marginally, and yields a
diaspora-per-capita coefficient of $-4.16$ ($p = 0.16$). The sign is the one the
diaspora mechanism predicts: countries with larger per-resident diasporas rank
better than population alone implies. The size of the effect is meaningful but the
significance is marginal at this $n$, and the additive specification (G2) is the
preferred reduced form. The interaction specification (G3) cannot be distinguished
from G1 plus noise.

The second result is Curaçao's location in the residual distribution. Computed
against the population-only specification, Curaçao's residual is $-79.8$ rank
places: actual rank 82 against a population-predicted rank of 162. The negative
sign indicates a country ranked considerably better than its resident population
alone predicts. Eighty places of residual is large in absolute terms; six countries
in the world have a more extreme over-ranking residual on this metric, and the six
are Uruguay, Croatia, Denmark, Portugal, Belgium and the Netherlands. Curaçao
ranks seventh in this residual distribution, ahead of Switzerland, Norway, Panama,
and Austria. We are not arguing that Curaçao is the football peer
of Croatia or Portugal. We are reporting that on the population-rank axis the
country occupies the same residual position as those federations.

The third result is the per-resident squad market value. The Curaçao men's squad
carries an aggregate Transfermarkt valuation of €28.7 million on 26 players. On
the World Bank WDI resident population of 155,967, the per-resident value is €184.
Iceland's per-resident value, computed identically on a WDI population of 386,506,
is €192. The two countries sit level on this measure on populations that differ
by a factor of 2.5. No other panel country reaches half of either figure. Cabo
Verde follows at €85, Suriname at €60, Luxembourg at €49 and Albania at €30
(figure 5). What this result says, in combination with the squad composition in
figure 2, is that the resident base of Curaçao is supporting a football pyramid
the size of Iceland's at forty per cent of the residents. The mechanism is the
diaspora-fed eligibility pool: every player in the senior squad develops in a
foreign professional league, and the weighted distribution of those leagues skews
toward European top-flight and second-tier teams. The squad is, in pyramid terms,
a Dutch-Turkish-German construction with a Caribbean badge.

```{r}
#| label: fig-per-capita-squad
#| fig-cap: "Transfermarkt squad market value per resident, thirteen-country focal panel. Curaçao at €184 per resident sits level with Iceland at €192, on a resident population forty per cent of Iceland's. Source: Transfermarkt 10 May 2026; World Bank WDI population (latest)."
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig5_per_capita_squad.png")
```

# Discussion

The first thing the result clears away is the framing that FIFA's ranking misses
small-state quality. It does not. At the global scale the ranking does most of what
population alone predicts, and the residuals against population are the analytically
meaningful object. Where the popular and academic discussion has gone is to read
small-state qualifications as ranking failures. The data point in the other
direction. For Curaçao specifically, the rank is roughly eighty places higher than
its 150,000 residents predict, and this is consistent with a squad whose per-resident
market value sits level with Iceland's. The rank is doing what the squad value implies.

One observation in this residual structure is worth surfacing because of how
Curaçao's qualification is read in Dutch public discussion. The Netherlands is
Curaçao's constitutional partner in the Kingdom of the Netherlands and the typical
reference point for how Dutch and Dutch-Caribbean audiences read Curaçao's place in
the football world. Both countries sit in the same residual cluster against
population. The Netherlands at 17.5 million gets there through a deep domestic
professional league and decades of academy investment. Curaçao at 150,000 gets there
through a diaspora-fed eligibility pool that draws on the same Dutch professional
pyramid. Different mechanism, same residual position.

What matters then is the squad. Curaçao's squad is, in
descriptive terms, a Dutch-Turkish-German-Saudi football pyramid carrying a Caribbean
flag. The pyramid is built from the diaspora-fed eligibility pool, with FIFA's
parent-and-grandparent rule and the senior-cap-tied switching option doing most of
the analytical work. For policy, this reframes the relevant question. Confederation
development funding tied to FIFA-rank improvement implicitly rewards the ranking
output without separating the squad-quality and federation-governance components that
generate it. The Curaçao case shows that for small states the squad component can be
imported almost in full from professional football in higher-tier countries; the
federation-governance question is then a different one, about how a federation
attracts, retains, and integrates diaspora-eligible players.

The result does not generalise to all small states with substantial diasporas, and
the data are explicit about why. Diaspora-per-capita is a marginally significant
predictor of rank residual ($p = 0.16$), not a robust one. The under-rankers at the
bottom of the residual distribution are concentrated in cricket-dominated South Asia
and in countries with disrupted football infrastructure, where the diaspora story is
not the binding constraint. We are documenting a strong descriptive pattern in a sub-class of states
where diaspora structure, FIFA eligibility rules and high-tier foreign professional
leagues align, not a universal mechanism. Curaçao is the most extreme case in the
data and the case the paper is built around.

A within-CONCACAF comparison sharpens the point. Jamaica sits in the same qualifying
confederation as Curaçao with a substantial UK-based diaspora drawing on the English
professional pyramid, a 0.40 diaspora-to-resident ratio, and an over-ranking residual
against population in the same direction as Curaçao's. Jamaica did not qualify in the
2026 cycle. The binary qualification outcome depends on contingencies that no
structural model captures: group draws, federation execution, the variance in how a
group of foreign-developed players performs together on a given day, the small
individual moments inside specific matches, and external shocks ranging from weather
events to injury timing. The diaspora-fed eligibility pool is necessary for the
squad-quality result we describe but not sufficient for the qualification outcome.
Small variations in any of these contingent factors could have produced a different
qualifying outcome, and a different paper.

A within-Kingdom case sharpens the same logic. All four men's and women's ABC squads
in our data operate under identical FIFA eligibility rules and can recruit from the
same Dutch professional football pyramid. The four outcomes vary substantially.
Curaçao men field a fully diaspora-trained squad and qualified for the 2026 World
Cup; Aruba men field fourteen island-based players and did not. The pattern flips on
the women's side. Aruba women beat Curaçao women 6-1 on 13 April 2026, the first
Aruban win in qualifier history and Curaçao women's fourth consecutive defeat.
Behind the result sits a federation choice: Aruba's women's team has been coached
since March 2024 by Arjan van der Laan, a former head coach of the Netherlands
women's national team, who has actively recruited Dutch-system players with Aruban
heritage. The Curaçao women's federation has not made the equivalent move. All four
squads sit inside the same Kingdom under identical rules with access to the same
host pyramid. What produces the variation is federation execution and pipeline
thickness.

Cape Verde provides a positive within-panel parallel. With a 525,000-person resident
population, a 0.37 diaspora-to-resident ratio on the UN DESA measure, a Cape Verdean
heritage-pool estimate from government and IOM sources that exceeds the resident
population, and a per-resident squad value of €85 (third in the panel after Curaçao
and Iceland), Cape Verde sits in the same structural sub-class on the descriptive
axes the paper foregrounds. The senior squad develops in the Netherlands, with a
long-standing Cape Verdean concentration in the Rotterdam area, and in the
Portuguese top flight. The mechanism is recognisable: a diaspora-fed eligibility pool
that extends the football pyramid beyond what the resident base alone supports. Cape
Verde occupies a structurally adjacent position on the descriptive axes the paper
foregrounds.

For research that uses national-team performance as a proxy for institutional or
human-capital outcomes in small states, the implication is methodological. The
ranking is a reasonable measure of national-team performance. National-team
performance, in turn, is a contaminated proxy for resident human capital where the
diaspora pool is large relative to the resident pool. The proxy mismeasures the unit
of analysis rather than being biased against it. For Curaçao a national-team result
captures the diaspora pyramid more than the resident base, and inference from the
rank back to the resident development outcome should be calibrated accordingly.

The framing connects to a long-running thread in the small-state-economics literature
on how resident-population denominators systematically mis-state the competitive
capacity of micro- and small-island economies and polities [@briguglio1995small;
@bertram2004convergence; @easterly2000small; @armstrong2003determinants;
@streeten1993microstates; @corbett2018democracy]. That literature has typically focused on income volatility,
trade openness, and MIRAB-style transfer dependence [@bertram1985mirab]; the
diaspora-as-eligibility-pool channel we identify is a different mechanism on the same
analytical axis. It is also consistent with the diaspora-economics literature that
treats diasporas as economic extensions of the sending country rather than as
brain-drain leakages [@docquier2012globalization; @beine2001brain; @gould1994immigrant;
@saxenian2006argonauts; @kapur2010diaspora]. The football pyramid is one of the few
empirical settings where this re-incorporation through a clearly-defined eligibility
rule is directly observable and quantifiable. The broader phenomenon almost certainly
operates in any domain where institutional eligibility rules and a deep host-country
professional infrastructure align. Three candidate domains are worth naming, because
they are the ones where small-state development outcomes are most commonly
mis-measured against the resident population. Academic publishing: a small-country
research output is partly the work of diaspora scientists trained at foreign
institutions and reattached to the home country through co-authorship and affiliation
rules that are themselves a form of eligibility. Medical specialism: the cardiologists,
oncologists and surgeons in a small-country tertiary hospital frequently trained in
Houston, Boston or Amsterdam, are licensed under home-country eligibility frameworks,
and produce health outcomes the resident professional base alone could not support.
Specialised business services: the law, audit and financial-engineering capacity of a
small country with a substantial professional diaspora draws on training and
credentials the resident base could not generate independently. In each case, the
resident-population unit understates the country's competitive capacity, for the same
reason it understates Curaçao's in football: the eligibility rules let host-country
infrastructure feed home-country output. The football pyramid is the most legible
case. The phenomenon is more general.

Two adjacent markets carry the same test, both in real time. Betting markets price
expected match outcomes; secondary ticket markets price expected demand for the
match experience. Both reveal what the public actually believes about Curaçao's
quality, and neither needs the FIFA ranking to do so. Dynamic pricing for the
14 June Curaçao-Germany opener has placed it among the most affordable group-stage
tickets at the tournament: resale prices around US\$300 against a tournament-wide
average that runs several times higher, with platforms framing the match as
attractive entry-level pricing because the on-paper mismatch suppresses demand.
Betting-market odds for the same match imply a Curaçao win probability that, on
the squad-value-implied quality of the side, is plausibly too low. The FIFA
ranking, as we have shown, captures most of what resident population predicts about
national-team quality. Whether betting markets and ticket markets do the same is a
separate question. If both systematically underestimate teams like Curaçao the way
resident-population metrics do, the under-measurement claim extends beyond the
ranking into public expectations more broadly, and the diaspora-fed pyramid becomes
a pricing anomaly as well as a measurement question. The empirical test we flag for
a multi-cycle deepening of the analysis combines betting-market closing odds, secondary
ticket-market prices, and realised match outcomes across small-state-with-diaspora
qualifications. The Curaçao-Germany ticket evidence is already in plain sight.

# Limitations

The cross-country regression rests on a single snapshot of FIFA rank from 10 May
2026. Rank is a slow-moving aggregate, but the full power of the relationship would
benefit from a time-series specification with country fixed effects across multiple
cycles, which we leave for a later iteration of the paper. The thirteen-country
focal panel for per-resident squad value is a curated convenience sample chosen for
descriptive contrast. Including or excluding individual countries shifts the
per-resident ranking but does not change the headline result that Curaçao and
Iceland sit at the top of the panel and other countries cluster well below them.

Transfermarkt squad market values are an imperfect proxy for realised quality. The
platform is the standard reference for transfer-market valuation in academic and
industry work [@coates2022wisdom; @herm2014crowd], but the values are
crowd-sourced from a community of contributors. They reflect expected fee on the
open transfer market rather than current performance, and for non-top-flight
players the variance widens. The aggregate per-squad number is more stable than any
individual valuation, but the per-resident finding for Curaçao would benefit from a
robustness check using alternative quality measures such as players' minutes at
top-tier clubs or a club-tier-weighted index.

The diaspora measure is the United Nations DESA total stock of country-of-origin
migrants resident outside the country. This is the standard cross-country measure
but it is a coarse instrument for what we actually want, which is the football
eligibility pool. The eligibility pool depends on FIFA's grandparent rule, on
residency conditions, and on senior-cap-tied switching. The first-generation migrant
stock under-counts the second- and third-generation descendants, which CBS Netherlands
and the Government of Cabo Verde track at substantially higher numbers for the
relevant Caribbean and African cases. We use the United Nations measure for
cross-country consistency and report the Netherlands-CBS heritage-pool figure
descriptively for the cases where it exists.

Selection on observed qualification is the relevant identification concern. We see
Curaçao at the residual we report partly because Curaçao qualified, and we cannot
test the counterfactual where Curaçao did not. A more demanding identification
strategy would compare the residual distribution across multiple qualification
cycles for the same set of small states. We do not undertake this here, and we
present the empirical pattern as descriptive and suggestive rather than as a causal
identification of the diaspora effect.

Finally, federation governance is part of the Curaçao story that this paper does not
attempt to model. Dick Advocaat led the team through the qualifying campaign and
resigned in February 2026 for family reasons. Fred Rutten succeeded him.
On 11 May 2026, less than five weeks before the opening match against Germany,
Rutten stepped down under sponsor and player pressure and Advocaat returned to take
the team to the tournament. Press chief Kees Jansma resigned over the reversal,
calling the federation's handling of the succession unacceptable and unjust.
The episode is governance turbulence at the worst possible time and will shape
on-field results during the tournament. It does not change the empirical object this
paper measures. The squad market value, the squad composition, and the
population-rank residual are calculated on the player pool and the rank as of 10
May 2026. If anything, what the drama makes visible is the institutional
architecture the paper documents. The departing head coach is Dutch. The returning
head coach is Dutch. The main sponsor whose stated preference materially shifted the
decision is Corendon, a Dutch travel firm. The playing group expressing the
coaching preference is rooted in the same Dutch professional culture as the coach
the federation reinstated. The competitive capacity Curaçao is bringing to the
United States in June is assembled across Dutch institutions, and the decision over
who walks the team out at NRG Stadium in Houston is governed by those same
institutions, in the same direction the rest of the paper documents.
The Federashon Futbol Korsou's recruitment of senior diaspora-eligible players over
the previous cycle and CONCACAF's qualifying-window scheduling decisions sit
upstream of the squad-quality and rank outcomes we observe. The mechanism this paper
describes is necessary but not sufficient for the conversion of a diaspora-fed
eligibility pool into a functional squad on the field; the governance work that
closes the gap is a separate analytical object, and the events of 11 May 2026
underscore how separate. We treat all of this as given. A federation-governance
specification of the residual, focused on how small federations make the conversion
from diaspora-eligibility-pool to fielded-squad, is the most natural candidate for a
follow-up paper.

# Conclusion

The case for reading Curaçao's qualification as anomalous against the FIFA ranking
does not survive the data. The ranking does close to what population alone predicts
across the world, and Curaçao's actual rank is consistent with the squad it is
fielding. The case for reading Curaçao's qualification as anomalous against its
resident population is stronger. The country sits in the seventh position of the
global residual distribution against population, alongside Uruguay, Croatia,
Denmark, Portugal, Belgium and the Netherlands. The mechanism that produces
this residual for the Curaçao case is the diaspora-fed eligibility pool, and
the squad's per-resident market value of €184 sits level with Iceland's €192
on forty per cent of Iceland's population.

The framing the data support is therefore a positive one. Small states with
substantial diasporas in countries that host deep professional football leagues can
assemble football pyramids that exceed what their resident population could carry on
its own. The FIFA ranking captures the quality of those pyramids in roughly the same
way it captures any other federation's. The question this paper carries beyond
football, into the diaspora-studies and migration-economics literatures, is how a
small state with a substantial diaspora produces a national output that the
resident-population frame cannot account for. Curaçao opens the 2026 World Cup
against Germany on 14 June with a squad that is, in pyramid terms, a multi-generation
diaspora given a Caribbean flag. The 150,000-person country and the 26-player squad
are the empirical anchor; the diaspora-as-national-capacity question is the
analytical one.

The football case is one of the cleanest empirical settings for a mechanism that
almost certainly operates elsewhere. Wherever institutional eligibility rules and
a deep host-country professional infrastructure align, the resident-population unit
will systematically understate what small states can produce. Curaçao's senior squad
is one observable, quantifiable instance of a broader claim about how small states
with substantial diasporas participate in larger economies through institutional
rules they share. The development-economics measurement gap this implies is not
restricted to football and may matter most in the domains where the diaspora is
not made visible by a single match on a single afternoon.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
